item.title: cotyledon
item.description:
A cotyledon is the first leaf or pair of leaves that emerges when a seed germinates. Cotyledons are not true leaves — they are embryonic structures packed inside the seed before germination, and they serve as the plant’s initial energy source before photosynthesis begins. In home gardening, the cotyledon stage is the primary harvest window for both sprouts and microgreens.

Most plants used for microgreens and sprouts are dicots (two cotyledons) or monocots (one cotyledon). Dicot cotyledons tend to be broad, tender, and mild-flavored — sunflower, radish, and broccoli microgreens are harvested at this stage. Monocot cotyledons, like those of wheatgrass, emerge as a single blade. The flavor, color, and texture of a microgreen at the cotyledon stage differ from the same plant harvested later, because true leaves develop different cell structures and chemical profiles.

item.title: food-safe
item.description:
Food-safe describes a material, surface, or substance that is suitable for contact with food without introducing harmful chemicals, residues, or contaminants. In home gardening — particularly when growing sprouts and microgreens — the food-safe status of containers, growing media, and cleaning agents determines whether the harvest is safe to eat.

Common food-safe considerations for home growers include: whether a plastic container is rated for food contact (look for recycling codes 1, 2, 4, or 5 and explicit food-grade labeling), whether a growing medium contains fertilizers or treatments not approved for edible crops, and whether cleaning products leave residues that could transfer to sprouts during the rinse cycle. Glass jars and stainless steel mesh are generally food-safe by default; plastics and coated metals require verification.

item.title: microgreen tray
item.description:
A microgreen tray is a shallow container used to grow microgreens in a thin layer of growing medium. The tray provides the structure that holds the medium, retains moisture, and supports the plants during their short growth cycle from seed to harvest.

Standard microgreen trays are flat-bottomed plastic or metal containers, typically 10 by 20 inches (the “1020” nursery flat), with a depth of one to two inches. Some trays have drainage holes for bottom-watering setups; others are solid for use as catch basins beneath a perforated growing tray. A common two-tray setup pairs a holed tray (holding soil and seeds) with a solid tray beneath (holding water that wicks upward through the drainage holes). This arrangement provides consistent moisture without overwatering the surface, which reduces mold risk.

item.title: rinse cycle
item.description:
A rinse cycle is a routine of rinsing seeds or sprouts with clean water to reduce microbial growth and maintain freshness during the growing process. In home gardening, rinse cycles are the primary food-safety intervention available to growers who lack commercial-scale sanitation equipment.

A typical rinse cycle involves draining standing water from the growing container, flooding it with fresh cool or lukewarm water, gently agitating the seeds or sprouts to dislodge loose seed hulls and bacterial biofilm, and draining again. For jar-method sprouts, this means inverting and draining the jar through a mesh lid. For tray-grown microgreens, the growing medium retains moisture and rinsing is replaced by misting or bottom-watering.

item.title: sanitize
item.description:
To sanitize is to reduce microbes on tools, jars, trays, or other equipment to a level considered safe for food-safe use. In home gardening for sprouts and microgreens, sanitizing is a routine step before each growing cycle because the warm, moist conditions that support germination also support bacterial growth.

Common sanitizing methods for home growing equipment include: soaking in a dilute bleach solution (one tablespoon per gallon of water, followed by thorough rinsing), washing with hot soapy water and air-drying, or using food-grade hydrogen peroxide. The choice depends on the material — glass and stainless steel tolerate bleach solutions well; some plastics degrade with repeated bleach exposure. Seeds themselves can be sanitized before soaking by brief immersion in a dilute hydrogen peroxide or vinegar solution, which reduces surface-borne pathogens without damaging the seed’s ability to germinate.

item.title: seed hull
item.description:
A seed hull is the outer shell of a seed, which often detaches during sprouting and must be removed before harvest. In home gardening for sprouts and microgreens, hulls are a practical concern because they affect texture, appearance, and shelf life of the finished product.

Hull behavior varies by seed type. Sunflower seed hulls are large, hard, and conspicuous — they cling to cotyledons and must be removed by hand or by rinsing under running water. Broccoli and radish hulls are smaller and often wash away during the rinse cycle without extra effort. Some seeds, like chia and flax, produce a mucilaginous coating when wet that makes hull separation difficult, which is one reason these seeds are less commonly sprouted.

item.title: sprout
item.description:
A sprout is a germinated seed harvested and eaten before true leaves develop, typically within three to seven days of soaking. In home gardening, sprouts are among the simplest crops to grow because they require no soil, no light (in most cases), and no specialized equipment beyond a jar and clean water.

The sprouting process begins with soaking seeds in water for several hours to initiate germination. After soaking, the seeds are drained and kept moist through regular rinse cycles — typically two to three times daily. The seed absorbs water, the seed hull splits, and a root and shoot emerge. Sprouts are harvested when the shoot has grown to one or two centimeters but before the cotyledons fully expand. Common sprouting seeds include mung bean, alfalfa, radish, broccoli, and lentil.
